title: Alastair Adams -Hiring Manager Screen | Client Operations Manager - Agency
meeting_title: Alastair Adams -Hiring Manager Screen | Client Operations Manager - Agency

transcript.text: Hi, Alastair.
Hi, Andi, how are you?
Good, how are you?
Yeah, not bad, thanks.
Where are you calling from?
I am in the middle of England.
I'm in, like, there's a county called Staffordshire, which is kind of between Manchester and London.
So I kind of commute either direction.
But, yeah, it's quite, it's, like, a really nice place to live.
There's a lot of countryside, but also it's really well connected.
Lovely.
Well, thank you for accommodating and happy to be chatting with you.
Good afternoon, I suppose, because you're in the feed from me.
Yeah, me too.
Yeah.
So I can kick us off by giving you a little bit of background on, like, myself and GrowthX, how I'm thinking about the role.
We'll spend some time talking about you.
Thank you.
And then I'll leave some time at the end.
This really shouldn't take 45 minutes.
It hasn't so far.
So, you know, we can end right at the 30-minute mark.
Okay.
But if you have questions, we can go a little over.
Yeah.
So I've been at GrowthX for seven months now.
We're a little over a year old.
We've just done our first round of, like, renewals and trying to figure that out.
Starting to measure client health, like, get a gauge on kind of, like, ICP.
Those are the things that are top of mind that we're thinking about from an operations standpoint.
As the head of client ops, I'm actually managing kind of the organization of the full delivery team.
So GrowthX is structured as a services agency.
So we're generating marketing content and organic blog content for our clients.
Kind of run the gamut of like all kinds of tech companies, etc.
But what we are on the back end is actually we're building our own product that generates content using AI powered workflows.
Eventually, that product will be external facing.
So we're at a really interesting time where we're trying to figure out how to like keep a cap on some of like our efficiency.
We're growing really quickly, which is amazing.
We're onboarding two clients a week.
But obviously, because we're services agency, these are super high touch clients.
So like our headcount is growing linearly as well.
But I think like eventually the goal is to become really like a product led company with a services arm for like our really core ICP, like niche, high profile clients.
What that means on the for like client ops and for this role is I've been spending most of my

Time right now thinking, as I said, about like capacity, headcount, who goes where, what's like the best sort of org structure to serve our clients, meet our deliverables, and then like not let anything drop while still, you know, like we're taking in money and it's great, but like we have to keep that sustainable for now.

Um, so that's been top of mind for me, I'm looking for someone who can help me, like, I, we want to implement like some better like project management and client health tracking tools, um, who can kind of help me on like the data and analysis side.

We're also, I think hiring a data analyst, like as part of this, but for now, you know, we've come up with some rudimentary like health scoring and ICP scoring, but pulling that all into one place, we're using like.
Notion, Linear for tracking tickets and projects.
We're using HubSpot for deals, but we don't have enough seats.
So this person will kind of help.
I think the initial on-the-ground focus will be tools and just kind of helping manage so I can take a step back and think more about the next steps.
But because we're about to make this big shift and thinking about the product as well, there are probably a million different ways that this specific role could grow and expand.
So I think that's the exciting part about this role.
You'll be stepping into an area where there's not a lot of definition.
We have a lot of ideas.
But we need somebody to help us just like get on the ground and execute.
And then also to kind of...
And you can think about, like, support if you want.
You can think about, like, keep thinking about ops and services.
There's a bunch of different directions as we grow that this could go.
So, like, I don't have a clear idea on career pathing, but the right person could kind of write their own job description for what that looks, this looks like in six, nine, 12 months.
Yeah.
Perfect.
Cool.
Any questions on that or follow-ups before we...
No, it's just, it's just scarily similar to the, to the job that I just had, actually.
Okay.
And that's what I liked about it.

The fact that I, I started from a point where the company was very, sort of, much smaller scale and then kind of path in a way that I could never have predicted and kind of ended up doing many different things and, and, like, learning all those things and, like, being able to kind of be the expert eventually.
And that's, like, it sounds very much like it's, like, pretty much like that state for you at the moment.
So, yeah, it's actually...
it's, it's Thank
That's the exciting part of a job for me.
So that's exactly what I kind of would want it to hear.
Amazing.
Well, I would love to then hear kind of your elevator pitch a little bit more about your experience.

Yeah, so I guess the most relevant is for the last three and a half years, pretty much what I just sort of outlined is that I was working at Handshake, which is like a SaaS platform, like an ed tech platform.
They're quite big in the US, so you've probably heard of them in the US.
But in the UK and in Europe, when I joined, they were kind of operating as a separate sort of startup within the company because there was no – obviously, the product didn't exist.
It had just been like kind of copy and pasted, and like they were hoping it could work in the UK.

So I started in a role which was like kind of more like an enablement role initially, which was managing like the support instance globally, but then also managing all of the training and documentation and the help centre, which at
I also noticed was part of this role description as well, which is quite nice.
But then pretty much I kind of, that role immediately expanded because there was nobody else doing operations.

It was kind of like not obviously initially a direct operations role, but within six months, it turned into, we need someone that can kind of start to manage Salesforce and like manage the CRM, set up like NPS and voice of customer surveys and start to do our data analysis.
So I learned a lot of stuff like the RevOps side and the data analysis side eventually became the things I was working with the most.
So like we used Looker, then I kind of learned a bit of SQL as well.

And then obviously I was using Salesforce quite a lot and Gainsight from the customer success side and doing a lot of automation, a lot of process improvements using things like Zapier and other automation systems.
I like
We also use Linear as well for issue tracking.
We still use it, actually.

So, yeah, mean, a lot of tooling, a huge amount of tooling that I ended up using and supporting all the go-to-market teams, like all of the functions, sales, success, support, and marketing with all of their systems.
And I was the only person doing that job in Europe, and it was about an 80 to 90-person team.
So, yeah, I did do a lot, and I was working, again, in a kind of similar structure to what this would be as well, which was one, Director of Operations, and then me, pretty much so.

So I really liked that kind of vibe because I was kind of, obviously, there was a bit of strategic thinking involved, but I was, like, mainly executing and kind of being able to kind of come up with the ideas and suggest.
And she was also able to kind of be more leadership level and kind of sort of collaborate with the US and collaborate with the European team as well.
So, yeah, mean, quite similar, I think, in terms of the scope and obviously what I...
I actually doing, although I feel like this obviously is a bit more focused maybe, but initially to do with the success side, not the sales side, which is probably the big difference.
Yeah, yeah.
Can you walk me through a workflow that you built end to end to improve delivery or customer success?
Yeah.
So within our success team, I used to do these kind of monthly meetings where I would talk to the, there was actually two separate success teams.
There was one because we had two different sides of the business.
had like a university success and the sales team.
And then we had like an employer, which is like a company.
And that was a big revenue generator.
So that was more important to focus on.
So the employer side, they used to do like monthly.

And then eventually they changed a quarter because of the scale business reviews where they would produce these slide decks and they would kind of like try and show how valuable the products had been and like how successful the partnership had been so far and just to prove value consistently.
And we had like two CSMs at this point, or two or three, and they were managing like sort of 80, I think at that point, about 80 customers.
it was a lot, so there's a lot of responsibility.
And they were just spending ages creating these decks, like going into, obviously I'd built a lot of reporting, a lot of dashboards that they were using.

But the blocker was like they were having to go in and like fill these decks in with this data every month and like refresh the data and like maybe add to it or refine it depending on the type of customer.
So I essentially just scoped out a tool that I found that we could maybe utilize and implement, which is called Matic, which is a, it's like a tool which plugs into the data software that you use.

So it was BigQuery, Google BigQuery, and then you basically can build reports and then basically on a sort of request basis, just like put the employer ID in and it would just like fill the whole deck with all the reporting.
Oh, wow.
How do you spell that?
M-A-T-I-K, MATIC.
So it integrates with a lot of different data sources.

But honestly, the difference it made, I can't even, it was more than 95% of time that they got freed up, because all they had to do was just click a button, basically, and it would just give them this deck, which they would have otherwise spent an hour per customer making from scratch.
So that's just one example.
And that meant we didn't actually have to hire another CSM for a bit longer, because they could just use the existing capacity that we had.
And it was just such a big difference.
And obviously, I mean, the cost of it, you have to have to buy the tool when I implement it and build it and everything.
It took a few months, but it was well, well, well worth it.
And like a big, probably one of the biggest things that I did that saved time and saved kind of money internally.
That's incredible.
I feel like you're actually looking I'm like looking for for it.
mean, that's not the problem we're facing, but I'm looking for, like, that kind of solution right now in terms of our scalability, and that's not a worry.
Okay, well, so as you sort of started to expand your role, I'm sure there was, like, a lot that you had listed of things that you wanted to improve.
So how did you think about, like, deciding which operational problems to solve first?
Hmm.

So I think the most important thing for me was, A, understanding, like, what's the, what are the most critical, like, revenue-side things in the business that, like, I could spend the most time and get the most benefit from.

Because at some point when I started, I was kind of, like, trying to do everything, and somebody gave me some advice, and they were just like, like, this isn't, look at actually what you're doing here.
It's probably saving, like, one person.
Yeah.
But it's not really been worth it.
So I think, like, it was just a case of, like, me getting good communications, like, getting to know a lot of different people.

Like, I literally was, like, I literally would speak to everyone and, like, and just understood pretty much everyone in leadership and, like, ICs, like, how they were thinking and, like, what they were working on and try to kind of just basically prioritise based on that because, like, the biggest impact things are the best things to work on.
And I don't think you can really know what the biggest impact things are without that kind of line of communication.
So to me, the most important thing in the business and what I learned was just, like, to keep, keep on top of, like, what, what is going on, like, be in touch with everyone.
Yeah.
Kind of be that person that, like, that somebody else could go to and be like, what's, like, what's your take on this?
Because you know everything, you know, you speak to everyone.
Yeah.
Kind of, yeah, it's just, it's just literally that really prioritising from there.
Yeah.
Yeah.
How did you think about
Like measuring your impact and telling that story on the other side.
Yeah, so it's actually really difficult, measuring the impact thing.
That was probably the most challenging thing.
And for a long time, there wasn't really any direct way that I could measure my impact because I was doing so many different things.
And obviously, the impact was kind of like internal.
And then it was like a lagging metric as to whether that was actually impacting the customer-facing side.
But obviously, like, I mean, the example I gave before of freeing up time, like that was a big thing.
But it's very, very difficult, obviously, to kind of quantitatively track how much time someone's saving as a result of your automation or your thing.

But yeah, I mean, it's something that I think you could probably, it would be interesting to work on, like to kind of come up with a solid methodology of like what number can you look at that like proves that you've been doing a good job.
Yeah.
That's directly attributable to you, not just like something you could say was like,

Like situational, like it might've already happened by itself or like, but yeah, I think, I think that this kind of role is very difficult to do that in, but like, it's also people have the gut instinct and like, you can just speak to people and they're like, this has definitely helped me.
Like, this is like hugely saved time or like this dashboard has like provided me data that I otherwise couldn't have got and otherwise couldn't have made that decision based on.
Um, so yeah, I think it's, I think it is a bit more qualitative, but like there is a few different numbers you could probably look at and like maybe draw a bit from.
Totally.
Um, as you were changing processes, um, how, like, and taking more things on, how did you think about like change management and adoption of like new SOPs?
Yeah, um, it is difficult, um, especially when you're trying to change a process that has been, um, in place for a long time.
I think my, my methodology was like, try and get, try and try.
Try and get things in place, like, as soon as possible.
Try and, like, make sure that you're making, like, if you're going to think about changing something, do it straight away.
Because, like, the longer that people are using things, the more difficult it is to kind of get them out of that habit.
But one example was, at some point, we were using Salesforce only for all of our sales and success.
And then we, it was actually an initiative that came from the US, but they basically wanted to implement.
Gainsight, which is, like, a customer success platform.
don't know if you've probably used it before, but it's just, like, an overlay for Salesforce.
And it's a bit more CSM-oriented.
And that was, like, a big project, because, obviously, all the CSMs were used to working in Salesforce and these other tools.

And we had to kind of work out a way to, like, change all of the way they were working and, like, change the way that they were inputting data and, like, everything, essentially, to get them to use this new tool.
And it took probably, like, a year in total.
total.
So,

Um, but it's just like a case of like, I think really good documentation, really good training, like making sure that you've got like, um, clear processes that you've set up, like, and that they're communicated well, because, um, that there's a lot of people that just don't document anything and just do like one call with someone and expect them to remember everything.
And then they're never going to do that because they're just, they're just not taking it in.
And then also making the processes that you put in place, like as simplistic as possible, and actually kind of trying to like, maybe automate things that were previously more labor, labor intensive.
And that's then an incentive in itself to kind of change and use the new process because it's actually saves time and it makes it easier.

So I guess a bit of like, kind of changing the mindset of, of, um, of people and like, kind of sell it to them as a way that like, you know, that these things have improved, like this is going to make your life easier, which most of the time, what all the time it should be doing.
So yeah, I think probably those things really, but yeah, it's situational as well.
That depends on what you're trying to change.
Yeah.
That makes sense.
Okay.
Last question.
Can you talk to me about a time you've worked through conflicting priorities between teams to get something done?
Yeah.
So in Handshake, actually had a lot of, because of the nature of the product, was like kind of US, originally US developed.
And so there was a product team in the US, and then eventually we got a product team in Europe, and they kind of slightly tailored some of the features more to Europe.

But there was obviously a lot of conflict, well, naturally there's a lot of conflict between like the go-to-market teams, because they're the ones that are selling the products, and they're being made promises by certain people, and like the customers, and then the product team themselves, and the engineers, because they're the ones that are trying.

So yeah, one example was, we had like a very difficult time, a lot of the time, like trying to explain certain processes, certain terminologies to the customers, just because it's quite a complicated product.
And this is probably a bit further away from maybe what this role is, but it just shows you what I was actually working with in Handshake.
So like, yeah, a long time that was the case.
So I met both teams quite regularly, obviously, because I spoke to everyone.
The product side, were kind of just like, they were doing translations, and they were doing like, they were regionalizing the platform as well.
So there was like different issues there.

And then obviously, on the go to market side, they were like, trying to explain very difficult concepts, which didn't, which actually detracted people from using the product because they didn't want to go in.
So, yeah, essentially the idea that I had was to create like an internal resource, which is like a big product terminology resource, which we could kind of build up from scratch.
And there was a lot of things which nobody had ever defined, like, what does this mean?
What's the exact definition of this, like this line in the platform?
doesn't, nobody knows what it means.
And it happened all the time.
There's just constantly things like that.
So like I worked with one of the product managers, like we had like sessions over a number of weeks, like we kind of, we like had a list of a lot of things.
And then we built up that list over that period and like built this huge resource and we put it into Confluence and then communicated it like together to the go-to-market teams.
And they were kind of like, I never even knew that this meant this, like this, like I didn't even know that was, uh, that was how you would define that.
Um, but obviously it made it much easier because then they could say, well.
In this reporting dashboard, what you're actually looking at here is this, specifically, not what you thought you were looking at before.

that's just, I mean, it's not really that complicated, to be honest, but it's just like a simple way of like sort of refining the way that people look at things and like kind of making people or making the teams more harmonious, guess, and like keeping that then up to date and like keeping it, yeah, sort of accurate was another thing.
But yeah, just one idea that I had, it's not really a complicated idea, but it did make a big difference because it never existed before.
And it was just something that kind of, you know, you could kind of cross-functionally build and it just makes a sort of small change to people's, to the way that the teams are working.
Yeah.
Okay.
Those are all my questions.
What questions do you have for me?
So I guess one of my, one of
Hi, big questions from, obviously you've been doing this for seven months.
Was there anyone, were you the third, yeah, I should guess you were the first person in this role, like, when it was hired for originally.
So you kind of built the team up from scratch, I guess.
Yeah, so I was, I report to, like, the VP of RevOps or something like that.
But she, yeah, she's very much on the finance side and, like, also kind of reports directly to the CEO and just thinks about, like, the business as a whole.
Probably our COO at some point.
The, because we don't have one of those now.
Okay.
And, yeah, we just didn't have anybody that was thinking, like, at a high level about, like, what the delivery org specifically looked like.
We have, obviously, a team of engineers.
They're pretty siloed.
And so.
That was one of the early things that I did coming in was as we started to develop, like, a client ops pool of engineers who could support, helping figure out, like, prioritization.
And then also just, like, I think we did a reorg in the first month that I joined of kind of focus and who's working on what clients.
We also, like, shifted to our internal platform.
We had been using air ops before then.
And so those were, like, some of the big things that I did coming in.
There wasn't anybody in this role before.
And so I think this is just, like, the expansion of the team slowly as we grow.
Yeah.
Okay, cool.
So then from your perspective, like, what kind of expectation do you have of, like, the kind of person when you've hired this role?
Like, what kind of person will be in it?

Like, do you want it to be, like, kind of how I've already described, like, the relationship I had before, where it's kind of, like, like, someone's, like, an executor more and then you're kind of doing the more of the strategic side?
Or would you want there to be a bit of strategic thinking in this role as well, like, and to be more tied in with, like, the leadership?
Yeah, I mean, I think to start, there's just things that, like, we need to execute on.
I think as we grow, like, one person thinking about all these problems, like, there's room for more, like, input strategic thought on that.
Like, yeah, I, like, I definitely am not going to say, like, I will be the sole decision maker.
I like that, I like a collaborative working environment.

And I think, like, especially given how fast we're growing, how much is changing, like, I want this person to feel empowered to say, like, see something, make, like, kind of do the decision tree, make the decision.
And as long as, you know, you can talk through, like, how you got there, like, for it, I'm not going to, I don't want to be a blocker in any way, because there's.
So much that has to be done.
Yeah, cool.
And then I guess the lead on question would be like, you've worked for, I noticed you worked for Uber before.
So like you, you've worked for quite a big company and like now you're in this role where you've kind of been having to be more, I guess, self-sufficient or like there's been a lot more pressure.
I just, I just wondered about how that's been for you, like how you felt that that's been.
Yeah, I mean, my time at Uber, I was in an incubator that was like a hot thing for a minute.
Oh, okay.
Startups where they had like their own little startups that they were like thinking about spinning off.
So like probably half of my time at Uber, the second half of my time at Uber was spent in an incubator where we basically like had our own budget engineers.
And it was like, this is the pot of money that you have, make it work or don't.
So that was like great in terms of like learning just to like execute and go make decisions and just.
Exactly what I just explained to you.
Yeah, yeah, yeah.
I think the frustrating part was it was still within the Uber product.
And so like, we could only get so far and then we'd be like, okay, this is ready.
And then like the team that owned the homepage of the app would be like, there's not space for you here.
Yeah, yeah.
Okay, that's interesting.
I didn't even know that was a thing.
Yeah.
That was the transit team.
So it was kind of cool.
were making deals with government transit authorities.
And so you could like buy a ticket for like a bus on the app or whatever.
Yeah, I noticed you can now like buy plane tickets on there and like, but you can do train tickets as well.
I just, it's interesting that they've gone down that route, I guess.
Yeah, yeah.
Trains was part of my team for a little while.
Yeah.
Interesting.
And then also, you mentioned that there was like a bit, you wanted someone who's like, you were hiring a day journalist, but you wanted someone who was a bit more like also data oriented, like.
Do you have like a BI tool that you're using at the moment, or is it like a kind of still something that's not been?
On the engineering side, we have Snowflake, like, and we're starting to collect data, like, on our internal platform, kind of what's, because we're putting a bunch of client information in there.
But in terms of reporting, right now, we mainly rely on our customers instances of GSC and GA4, and we put them into Looker.
There's like work that can be done to standardize that.

don't know if that's like the P0 of this, you know, but I like, I think somebody that can help just kind of evaluate, make those connections, and that is able to, like you said, you know, that piece about having dabbled in SQL is amazing, because sometimes you just need to like, pull that, make a connection, whatever.
So it's great.
you.
Thank
Okay, cool.
Interesting.
Well, I mean, I don't have any more questions, but I just really like the sound of this role.
I actually think that the more that you've spoken about it, the more it sounds like something that I would be really interested in doing, or I would really enjoy doing as well.
Awesome.
Yeah.
Well, I'm going to move you on.
So I think you'll talk to Bridget next.
And yeah, it was great chatting with you.
So looking forward to connecting again.
Okay.
Thanks, Andi.
Appreciate it.
Have a good rest of the day.
Bye.

default_summary.markdown_formatted:
## Meeting Purpose

[Screening Alastair Adams for the Client Operations Manager role.](https://fathom.video/share/7C98iEb8x3bmynz4A4sJqeb96bdz96Aa?tab=summary&timestamp=112.0)

## Key Takeaways

  - [**Strong Fit:** Alastair's Handshake experience directly matches the role's needs: building ops from scratch, managing tools (Salesforce, Gainsight, Looker), and automating workflows.](https://fathom.video/share/7C98iEb8x3bmynz4A4sJqeb96bdz96Aa?tab=summary&timestamp=426.0)
  - [**Proven Impact:** At Handshake, Alastair automated business review decks with Matic, saving CSMs \>95% of their time (1 hour/deck) and deferring a new hire.](https://fathom.video/share/7C98iEb8x3bmynz4A4sJqeb96bdz96Aa?tab=summary&timestamp=612.0)
  - [**GrowthX's Challenge:** Rapid growth (2 new clients/week) is straining high-touch services, creating a linear headcount problem that requires immediate operational efficiency.](https://fathom.video/share/7C98iEb8x3bmynz4A4sJqeb96bdz96Aa?tab=summary&timestamp=197.0)
  - [**Role's Scope:** The role is undefined, focusing initially on tools (Notion, Linear, HubSpot) and data to free up Andi for strategy. It offers the right candidate the chance to "write their own job description."](https://fathom.video/share/7C98iEb8x3bmynz4A4sJqeb96bdz96Aa?tab=summary&timestamp=311.0)

## Topics

### GrowthX's Operational Challenge

  - [**Business Model:** Services agency creating marketing content for tech clients.](https://fathom.video/share/7C98iEb8x3bmynz4A4sJqeb96bdz96Aa?tab=summary&timestamp=166.0)
  - [**Strategic Shift:** Evolving into a product-led company with a services arm for high-profile clients.](https://fathom.video/share/7C98iEb8x3bmynz4A4sJqeb96bdz96Aa?tab=summary&timestamp=218.0)
  - [**Core Problem:** Rapid growth (2 new clients/week) is straining high-touch services, causing headcount to grow linearly.](https://fathom.video/share/7C98iEb8x3bmynz4A4sJqeb96bdz96Aa?tab=summary&timestamp=197.0)
  - [**Current State:**](https://fathom.video/share/7C98iEb8x3bmynz4A4sJqeb96bdz96Aa?tab=summary&timestamp=233.0)
      - [**Tools:** Disconnected systems (Notion, Linear, HubSpot).](https://fathom.video/share/7C98iEb8x3bmynz4A4sJqeb96bdz96Aa?tab=summary&timestamp=264.0)
      - [**Data:** Rudimentary client health/ICP scoring; no standardized reporting.](https://fathom.video/share/7C98iEb8x3bmynz4A4sJqeb96bdz96Aa?tab=summary&timestamp=264.0)
      - [**Team:** Andi (Head of Client Ops) is focused on org structure and capacity, needing an executor to manage day-to-day ops.](https://fathom.video/share/7C98iEb8x3bmynz4A4sJqeb96bdz96Aa?tab=summary&timestamp=233.0)

### Alastair's Relevant Experience (Handshake)

  - [**Role:** Built the UK/EU ops function from scratch for Handshake's ed-tech SaaS platform.](https://fathom.video/share/7C98iEb8x3bmynz4A4sJqeb96bdz96Aa?tab=summary&timestamp=426.0)
  - [**Scope:** Sole ops person for an 80–90 person team, supporting all GTM functions (Sales, Success, Support, Marketing).](https://fathom.video/share/7C98iEb8x3bmynz4A4sJqeb96bdz96Aa?tab=summary&timestamp=544.0)
  - [**Tooling Expertise:** Salesforce, Gainsight, Looker, SQL, Zapier, Linear.](https://fathom.video/share/7C98iEb8x3bmynz4A4sJqeb96bdz96Aa?tab=summary&timestamp=491.0)

### Case Study: Automating Business Reviews

  - [**Problem:** CSMs spent 1 hour/customer manually creating monthly business review decks.](https://fathom.video/share/7C98iEb8x3bmynz4A4sJqeb96bdz96Aa?tab=summary&timestamp=640.0)
  - [**Solution:** Implemented Matic, a tool that auto-populates decks from data sources (e.g., BigQuery).](https://fathom.video/share/7C98iEb8x3bmynz4A4sJqeb96bdz96Aa?tab=summary&timestamp=693.0)
  - [**Impact:**](https://fathom.video/share/7C98iEb8x3bmynz4A4sJqeb96bdz96Aa?tab=summary&timestamp=693.0)
      - [Saved CSMs \>95% of their time.](https://fathom.video/share/7C98iEb8x3bmynz4A4sJqeb96bdz96Aa?tab=summary&timestamp=693.0)
      - [Deferred the need to hire another CSM.](https://fathom.video/share/7C98iEb8x3bmynz4A4sJqeb96bdz96Aa?tab=summary&timestamp=749.0)

### Operational Philosophy

  - [**Prioritization:** Focus on high-impact, revenue-critical problems. This requires deep communication with all teams to understand their needs.](https://fathom.video/share/7C98iEb8x3bmynz4A4sJqeb96bdz96Aa?tab=summary&timestamp=801.0)
  - [**Change Management:**](https://fathom.video/share/7C98iEb8x3bmynz4A4sJqeb96bdz96Aa?tab=summary&timestamp=993.0)
      - [**Incentivize:** Make new processes simpler and more automated to drive adoption.](https://fathom.video/share/7C98iEb8x3bmynz4A4sJqeb96bdz96Aa?tab=summary&timestamp=1106.0)
      - [**Document:** Provide clear SOPs and training; don't rely on single calls.](https://fathom.video/share/7C98iEb8x3bmynz4A4sJqeb96bdz96Aa?tab=summary&timestamp=1005.0)
  - [**Conflict Resolution:**](https://fathom.video/share/7C98iEb8x3bmynz4A4sJqeb96bdz96Aa?tab=summary&timestamp=1149.0)
      - [**Example:** Resolved go-to-market vs. product team conflict over confusing terminology.](https://fathom.video/share/7C98iEb8x3bmynz4A4sJqeb96bdz96Aa?tab=summary&timestamp=1149.0)
      - [**Solution:** Created a shared "Product Terminology Resource" in Confluence to standardize definitions.](https://fathom.video/share/7C98iEb8x3bmynz4A4sJqeb96bdz96Aa?tab=summary&timestamp=1149.0)

### Role Expectations & Growth

  - [**Initial Focus:** Execution on tools and data to free up Andi for strategy.](https://fathom.video/share/7C98iEb8x3bmynz4A4sJqeb96bdz96Aa?tab=summary&timestamp=264.0)
  - [**Decision-Making:** Empowered to make decisions and drive initiatives, provided the rationale is clear.](https://fathom.video/share/7C98iEb8x3bmynz4A4sJqeb96bdz96Aa?tab=summary&timestamp=1480.0)
  - [**Career Path:** Undefined, offering the right candidate the chance to "write their own job description" as the company evolves.](https://fathom.video/share/7C98iEb8x3bmynz4A4sJqeb96bdz96Aa?tab=summary&timestamp=322.0)

## Next Steps

  - [**Andi:** Move Alastair to the next interview stage.](https://fathom.video/share/7C98iEb8x3bmynz4A4sJqeb96bdz96Aa?tab=summary&timestamp=1742.0)
  - [**Alastair:** Interview with Bridget.](https://fathom.video/share/7C98iEb8x3bmynz4A4sJqeb96bdz96Aa?tab=summary&timestamp=1760.0)

action_items.description: Schedule next interview w/ Bridget + Alastair re: Client Operations Manager
